title: Rush seat drop-in panel
subtitle: Replacing a drop-in chair seat with a hand-woven rush panel
excerpt: Rush seating is the oldest form of chair seating still in common use in Britain: twisted rush cord woven in a figure-of-eight pattern across a simple seat frame. A drop-in seat panel is the easiest entry point because the frame is removable and the weaving can be done on a bench rather than while the chair is upside down. This intermediate project covers twisting rush cord, filling a square drop-in frame, and fitting the finished panel back into the chair.
sourceNotes: Encyclopaedia Britannica 11th edition (1911) for rush seating history and technique. Cassell's Household Guide (1869) for the domestic use and maintenance of rush-seated chairs.

glossaryTerms.definition: A twisted rope made from lengths of dried bulrush (Typha latifolia or Scirpus lacustris). Two or three rush lengths are twisted together as they are woven across the seat frame.
A removable seat panel that sits in a rebate in the chair frame and can be lifted out without tools. The most common form of upholstered or woven seat in dining chairs.

content.text: Rush seating uses twisted bundles of dried rush woven in a figure-of-eight pattern that builds a dense, cushioned surface directly on the seat frame. A
drop-in seat
frame can be woven on a bench top, which is far easier than weaving a fixed seat on a chair that must be held upside down throughout. The only material is
rush cord
twisted as you go; no special tools are needed beyond a knife to trim ends.
What you need: materials

Dried bulrush (Typha latifolia) or sea grass cord. For a 40 cm by 40 cm drop-in frame, approximately 1.5 to 2 kg of dried rush is needed. Dried rush is available from craft suppliers year-round. Harvested rush must be cut in summer and dried for at least two months. Soak dried rush in cold water for 5 minutes before use, then wrap in a damp cloth for 20 minutes to make it flexible without being waterlogged.
What you need: tools

A sloyd knife to trim rush ends. A length of thick string or a peg to hold the rush bundle when joining lengths. Padding material (crumpled newspaper or dried rush scraps) to stuff the corner triangles as they build up.
Safety
Cut rush ends away from the body. Rush edges are sharp when dry; work with dampened rush to avoid cuts.
The work
Step 1: Prepare the frame

Remove any old webbing, wadding, or staples from the drop-in frame. The frame should be a bare wooden square or rectangle with four rails. If the rails are rounded on top, leave them; if they have sharp edges, sand lightly so the rush cord does not cut against the rail edge.
Step 2: Twist and start the cord

Take three to four lengths of dampened rush, each 60 to 80 cm long. Hold one end together and twist the bundle clockwise so that a cord forms. Tie the cord end around the left rail near a corner. Maintain the twist by rolling the rush bundle between fingers and thumb as you weave; an untwisted rush bundle flattens and loses its cushioned quality.
Step 3: Figure-of-eight pattern

From the tied corner, bring the cord across the near rail, under the rail, up the inside face of the rail, and across the adjacent side rail in the same figure-of-eight motion. This wraps each corner of the seat in two directions simultaneously. Continue around all four corners in sequence: each full round of the four corners advances the pattern by one cord width on each rail. Pack each completed round toward the corner with hand pressure. As the bundle runs short, splice in new rush lengths by knotting them to the existing bundle on the underside.
Step 4: Fill and pad the corner triangles

As the pattern builds toward the centre, corner triangles of open space form under the surface weave. Stuff these with crumpled newspaper or dried rush scraps to prevent the seat from collapsing into these voids when in use. Insert the stuffing from the underside before the final rows close the opening.
Step 5: Fill the centre and finish

The last few rows narrow to a small gap in the centre. Weave the final length back and forth across this gap until it is filled, then push the loose end through to the underside, knot it, and trim with the knife. Let the seat dry for 24 hours before fitting; the rush will tighten slightly as it dries.
Variations
Sea grass cord is easier to work with than rush and requires no soaking. The surface is slightly coarser but very durable.

A rectangular seat needs a different technique for the longer rails: extra rows are woven across the long axis only until the seat becomes square, then the figure-of-eight continues around all four rails to close.
Care
Spray with water occasionally to keep the rush supple and prevent brittleness. A well-made rush seat lasts 20 to 30 years with normal use.

items.symptom: The surface looks lumpy rather than flat
items.cause: The rush cord was not twisted tightly enough, or adjacent rows were not packed together firmly.
items.fix: Keep a firm, consistent twist on the cord throughout. Pack each row toward the corner with both hands before starting the next round.

items.symptom: The seat is loose or springy after drying
items.cause: The rush was too wet when woven, or not packed firmly enough.
items.fix: A slightly loose new seat is normal; it will tighten further as it dries over the first week in use. If it remains loose after a week, the seat can be removed and an extra length of rush threaded through the underside wraps.
